message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aims</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We investigate mechanisms for potential pro-arrhythmic effects of hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) alone, or combined with azithromycin (AZM), in Covid-19 management supplementing the limited available experimental cardiac safety data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We integrated patch-clamp studies utilizing In Vitro ProArrhythmia Assay (CiPA) Schema IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    paradigms, molecular modelling, cardiac multi-electrode array and voltage (RH237) mapping, ECG studies, and Ca
                    <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                    (Rhod-2 AM) mapping in isolated Langendorff-perfused guinea-pig hearts with human in-silico ion current modelling.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    HCQ blocked I
                    <jats:sub>Kr</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>K1</jats:sub>
                    with IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    s (10±0.6 and 34±5.0 μM) within clinical therapeutic ranges, I
                    <jats:sub>Na</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>CaL</jats:sub>
                    at higher IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    s, leaving I
                    <jats:sub>to</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>Ks</jats:sub>
                    unaffected. AZM produced minor inhibition of I
                    <jats:sub>Na</jats:sub>
                    , I
                    <jats:sub>CaL</jats:sub>
                    , I
                    <jats:sub>Ks</jats:sub>
                    , and I
                    <jats:sub>Kr</jats:sub>
                    ,, sparing I
                    <jats:sub>K1</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>to</jats:sub>
                    . HCQ+AZM combined inhibited I
                    <jats:sub>Kr</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>K1</jats:sub>
                    with IC
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    s of 7.7±0.8 μM and 30.4±3.0 μM, sparing I
                    <jats:sub>Na</jats:sub>
                    , I
                    <jats:sub>CaL</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>to</jats:sub>
                    . Molecular modelling confirmed potential HCQ binding to hERG. HCQ slowed heart rate and ventricular conduction. It prolonged PR, QRS and QT intervals, and caused prolonged, more heterogeneous, action potential durations and intracellular Ca
                    <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                    transients. These effects were accentuated with combined HCQ+AZM treatment, which then elicited electrical alternans, re-entrant circuits and wave break. Modelling studies attributed these to integrated HCQ and AZM actions reducing I
                    <jats:sub>Kr</jats:sub>
                    and I
                    <jats:sub>K1</jats:sub>
                    , thence altering cell Ca
                    <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                    homeostasis.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Combined HCQ+AZM treatment exerts pro-arrhythmic ventricular events by synergetically inhibiting I
                    <jats:sub>Kr</jats:sub>
                    , I
                    <jats:sub>Ks</jats:sub>
                    with resulting effects on cellular Ca
                    <jats:sup>2+</jats:sup>
                    signalling, and action potential propagation and duration. These findings provide an electrophysiological basis for recent FDA cardiac safety guidelines cautioning against combining HCQ/AZM when treating Covid-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Mechanistic insights into ventricular arrhythmogenesis of hydroxychloroquine and azithromycin for the treatment of COVID-19
